q: Which BEST describes the relationship between AI, ML, deep learning, and agentic AI?
o: AI is broadest; ML learns from data; deep learning uses neural networks; agentic AI autonomously plans and executes multi-step tasks
Agentic AI is the broadest category
e: AI is the umbrella. ML learns patterns. Deep learning uses multi-layer neural networks. Agentic AI builds autonomous systems.

q: A company detects fraudulent transactions in real-time. Which inferencing type?
e: Real-time inferencing provides immediate low-latency predictions.

q: Which TWO scenarios indicate AI/ML is NOT appropriate? (Select TWO)
e: AI/ML is wrong for deterministic calculations and simple rule-based logic.

q: A healthcare company needs auditable explanations for high-risk patient flags. Which approach?
e: Traditional ML provides clear feature attribution for regulated explainability.

q: Which service covers the entire ML lifecycle?
e: SageMaker AI provides end-to-end ML lifecycle management.
Transcribe=speech-to-text. Polly=text-to-speech. Textract=doc extraction. Translate=translation.

q: Which concept describes repeatable processes and production readiness for ML?
e: MLOps: pipelines, monitoring, re-training workflows.

q: Model gets 95% on training, 60% on test data. What is happening?
e: Large training/test gap = overfitting.
Precision = TP/(TP+FP). Minimizes false positives.

q: Cost per user, ROI, customer feedback are examples of:
e: Business metrics measure value delivered.

q: Which learning type uses labeled input-output pairs?
e: Supervised = labeled data.

q: Amazon Q is BEST described as:
o: AI assistant for coding, AWS questions, and business tasks
e: Amazon Q = AI assistant.
Kiro is a developer tool alongside Strands Agents.

o: AI interpreting visual info from images/videos
e: Computer vision: object detection, facial recognition, medical imaging.

o: AI understanding and generating human language
e: NLP: chatbots, translation, sentiment analysis, summarization.

o: Computing system inspired by biological neurons
e: Interconnected nodes in layers learning complex patterns.

o: Using a trained model to predict on new data
e: Inference applies trained model to new inputs.
Clustering, anomaly detection, dimensionality reduction.

o: Agent learns by rewards/penalties from environment
e: Trial and error, reward signals, policy optimization.

q: Model performs poorly on BOTH training and test. This is:
e: Equal poor performance = underfitting (too simple).

o: Harmonic mean of precision and recall
e: F1 = 2*(precision*recall)/(precision+recall).

o: Area Under ROC curve measuring classifier performance across thresholds
e: Higher AUC = better discrimination. 0.5 = random.

o: Artificially expanding training data with modified versions
e: Rotate/flip images, add noise, synonyms. Reduces overfitting.

o: Splitting data into folds for robust performance estimates
e: K-fold provides reliable estimates vs single split.

o: Image/video analysis including object detection and facial analysis
e: Rekognition: objects, faces, text, content moderation, PPE.

o: NLP service extracting sentiment, entities, key phrases
e: Comprehend: sentiment, entities, key phrases, language detection.
Forecast: demand, inventory, workforce, revenue prediction.

o: ML service for real-time personalized recommendations
e: Same tech as Amazon.com recommendations.

o: Table of TP, TN, FP, FN for classification
e: Basis for precision, recall, accuracy, F1 calculations.

o: Processing large volumes through a model on schedule
e: Cost-effective for non-time-sensitive predictions.

o: Statistical relationship between inputs/outputs changes over time
e: Patterns evolve. Requires retraining to maintain accuracy.
Lex: chatbots with NLU. Same tech as Alexa.

o: Predicting which category an input belongs to
e: Spam/not spam, cat/dog, sentiment. Discrete categories.
Predict price, temperature, revenue. Continuous output.

o: Grouping similar data without predefined labels
e: Customer segmentation, document grouping. Unsupervised.

o: Root Mean Squared Error for regression models
e: Lower = better. Penalizes large errors more than MAE.

o: Ranking which inputs most influence predictions
e: Shows which inputs drive predictions. Explains models.

o: Tracking model performance in production for degradation
e: Track accuracy, data, concept drift. SageMaker Model Monitor.

o: Intelligent enterprise search powered by ML
e: Kendra: NLP-powered enterprise search across data sources.

o: Cleaning and transforming raw data for model training
e: Handle missing values, normalize, encode, remove outliers.

o: Protocol standardizing how agents connect to external systems/tools
e: MCP standardizes agent-to-tool connections for interoperability.

q: Which THREE are agentic AI concepts? (Select THREE)
e: Agentic AI: multi-agent patterns, memory, tools, orchestration, MCP.
Charged per token. Longer = more expensive.

o: Strategically designing info given to model for better output
e: Prompt design + RAG strategy + memory + relevance filtering.

o: Self-attention processes all positions in parallel
e: Self-attention enables massive parallelization over RNNs.

q: Same input producing different outputs is:
e: Probabilistic sampling = varying outputs.
Business value metrics vs model performance metrics.

o: Bedrock=FM API, Strands=agent framework, JumpStart=pre-built ML, AgentCore=agent security
e: Bedrock=FM access. Strands=agents. JumpStart=models. AgentCore=security.

o: Dense vectors where similar concepts are close
e: Semantic similarity = geometric proximity in vector space.

o: Generating false content presented as true
e: Plausible but wrong. Mitigate: RAG, validation, scoring.
Data > Model > Pre-training > Fine-tuning > Deployment.
Agents coordinate to solve complex problems.

o: Token pricing vs provisioned throughput vs custom models
e: On-demand vs reserved vs custom. Different cost/perf profiles.
Teacher > student at lower cost/latency.
Limits total I/O. Larger = longer docs but more cost.
Amazon's foundation models on Bedrock.

o: Large model trained on broad data adaptable to many tasks
e: FMs: massive training, adaptable via prompting/fine-tuning.

o: Initial training on massive data to learn general patterns
e: Learning general knowledge from billions of tokens.

o: Further training on domain data to specialize
e: Adapts general FM to specific tasks with targeted data.

o: Generates images by reversing noise process
e: Add noise in training, denoise in generation. DALL-E, Stable Diffusion.

o: Breaking text into units (tokens) for model processing
e: Models process tokens not raw text. Affects cost and limits.

o: Neural component weighing importance of input parts
e: Lets models focus on relevant parts. Enables parallel processing.

o: Applying knowledge from one task to a related task
e: Reuse pre-trained knowledge. Fine-tuning is transfer learning.

o: Models processing multiple data types: text, images, audio, video
e: Handle text+images+audio+video. GPT-4V, Claude vision.

o: Performing tasks without examples in the prompt
e: Model uses general knowledge. No examples needed.

o: Providing examples in prompt to guide behavior
e: 2-5 examples show desired format. Improves consistency.

o: Hidden instructions defining model behavior and constraints
e: Sets rules, persona, boundaries. Persists across turns.

o: Combining retrieval with generation for grounded responses
e: Retrieve docs, inject context, generate grounded answers.

o: DB optimized for storing/searching embeddings via similarity
e: Store embeddings, find similar via distance. Powers RAG.

o: Search understanding meaning/intent not just keywords
e: Query to embedding, find similar. Understands synonyms.

o: Greedy decoding always selecting most probable token
e: Most deterministic. Good for factual/code tasks.

o: Sampling from tokens covering cumulative probability p
e: Top-p=0.9 = sample from 90% cumulative probability.

o: Managed service for multiple FMs via single API
e: Serverless, multi-provider, unified API, no infra.

o: Managed RAG: auto chunk, embed, index, retrieve
e: End-to-end RAG pipeline. Auto-handles entire flow.

q: What makes agentic AI different from standard GenAI?
o: Autonomously plans, uses tools, maintains memory, executes multi-step workflows
e: Autonomous planning + tools + memory vs just generating responses.

o: Open-source framework for building agents with tools and multi-agent
e: Build agents that plan, use tools, collaborate.

o: Reserved capacity for consistent performance at committed volume
e: Lower per-token cost but requires commitment.

o: Time between request and complete response
e: Smaller models = lower latency. Tradeoff with quality.
Content filters, topic restrictions, PII redaction.

o: Techniques reducing cost/latency: quantization, caching, batching
e: Quantization, pruning, distillation, batching, caching.

o: Pre-built ML solutions and models with one-click deploy
e: Pre-trained models, solution templates, fast deployment.

q: Which THREE are FM selection criteria? (Select THREE)
e: Cost, modality, latency, multi-lingual, size, complexity, caching.
Low=focused. High=creative. Zero=greedy decoding.

q: Proprietary docs without retraining. Which approach?
e: RAG retrieves and provides context. No retraining.

q: Vector DBs: OpenSearch, Aurora, Neptune, RDS
e: Each has different vector implementation and strengths.
Prompting (free) < RAG < Fine-tuning < Pre-training.

o: Reusing computed representations for repeated prefixes
e: Same prefix = skip computation = lower cost/latency.

o: Instructions on what NOT to include
e: Exclude unwanted content in generation.

q: Which THREE are prompt security risks? (Select THREE)
e: Injection, jailbreaking, hijacking. Also: exposure, poisoning.
Version, A/B test, manage templates.
Improves zero-shot instruction following.

o: Training further on domain data without starting over
e: Extend knowledge without full retraining cost.

o: Using LLM to evaluate another model's outputs
e: Scales evaluation beyond human review.
ROUGE = summaries. BLEU = translation.
Capability fine but experience lacks.

q: Fine-tune vs RAG: when to fine-tune?
e: Fine-tune = HOW. RAG = WHAT.

o: Autonomously execute multi-step tasks via tools
e: Plan steps, call tools, synthesize results.
Retrieval precision/recall + generation faithfulness.
Improves math, logic, multi-step reasoning.

o: Human preferences guide model behavior improvement
e: Humans rank outputs, train reward model, RL aligns model.

o: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning with small adapter layers
e: Freeze base, train small matrices. Cheaper than full fine-tuning.

o: Breaking complex tasks into sequential prompts
e: Output of step 1 feeds step 2. Better results on complex tasks.

o: Defined tools/APIs an agent can invoke
e: Each group defines parameters and actions the agent can use.

o: Breaking docs into pieces for embedding and retrieval
e: Too large = diluted. Too small = lost context. Balance matters.
Keyword (exact) + semantic (meaning) = better retrieval.

o: Separate model re-scores retrieved docs by relevance
e: Initial retrieval + reranker = better context quality.

o: Connecting outputs to verifiable sources for accuracy
e: Model cites sources, stays faithful. RAG enables grounding.

o: Evaluates text using embeddings for semantic similarity
e: Better than BLEU/ROUGE for capturing paraphrases.

o: Evaluates translation quality via n-gram precision
e: Precision-oriented. Compares against human reference.

o: Metric measuring how well LLM predicts text; lower = better
e: How surprised model is. Lower = better prediction.

o: Extractive pulls exact sentences; abstractive generates new ones
e: Extractive selects. Abstractive creates novel summary.

o: Model calls external functions/APIs with correct parameters
e: Model decides function, generates params, executes, uses results.

o: Converts text/images to vectors for similarity search
e: Separate from generation. Used for RAG retrieval.

o: Amazon-built FMs on Bedrock for text and embeddings
e: Text generation, embeddings, image. Bedrock exclusive.

o: Reducing precision (32-bit to 8-bit) for speed with minimal quality loss
e: Smaller, faster, less memory. Good for edge/mobile.
Better UX, lower perceived latency.

o: System sets rules (hidden); user is actual query
e: System: developer rules. User: end-user input each turn.
Attacker tricks model. Mitigate: validation, guardrails.

o: Date after which model has no training data
e: Model can't answer about events after cutoff. RAG solves this.

q: What is A/B testing for FMs?
o: Comparing two configs with real users to measure which is better
e: Split traffic, measure metrics. Validates changes.

o: Systematically compare FM performance with automated/human methods
e: Compare models on your tasks. Informs selection.

o: RAG, low temperature, validation, citations to reduce false info
e: Layer: RAG + low temp + validation + citation + human review.

q: What is model selection for GenAI?
o: Evaluate cost, latency, quality, modality, context for task fit
e: Match model to requirements across multiple dimensions.

q: Which THREE are legal risks of GenAI? (Select THREE)
e: IP, trust loss, biased outputs, hallucinations as fact.

o: Consider energy/carbon and choose right-sized models
e: Bigger = more energy. Choose appropriate size.

o: Detects bias with SHAP, explains predictions, monitors drift
e: Bias detection + SHAP explanations + production monitoring.

o: Human review workflows for ML predictions
e: Routes low-confidence to human reviewers.
Transparency: intended use, limitations, demographics.

o: Filter harmful content, block topics, redact PII
e: Content filters + denied topics + PII redaction.

o: Simple = explainable but less accurate; complex = powerful but opaque
e: Decision tree vs deep learning tradeoff.

o: Historical data reflects biases, model perpetuates
e: Biased data = biased model.
User feedback + transparency + disclosure.

o: AI not disadvantaging groups based on protected characteristics
e: Equal treatment regardless of demographics.

o: Understanding why a model made a specific decision
e: Feature attribution, SHAP, decision paths.

o: Interpretability = inherently clear; explainability = post-hoc techniques
e: Decision tree (interpretable) vs SHAP on deep learning (explainable).

o: Neutral system producing disproportionate outcomes for a group
e: Even without intent, outcomes differ. Must test.

o: Review, override, correct AI decisions in high-stakes scenarios
e: High-risk decisions need human accountability.
Hate, threats, harassment. Guardrails filter these.

o: Risk tiers: unacceptable, high, limited, minimal
e: Different requirements per risk level.

o: Clear responsibility for AI outcomes and impacts
e: Who is responsible when AI errs? Clear ownership needed.

q: What is red teaming for AI?
o: Adversarial testing for vulnerabilities, biases, failures
e: Find jailbreaks, biases, safety gaps before deployment.

o: Invisible signals identifying content as AI-generated
e: Statistical patterns help detect AI content.

o: Automated systems blocking harmful categories in model I/O
e: Classify I/O into harm categories, block by severity.

o: Citing documents used to generate response for verification
e: Shows sources. Users verify. RAG enables this.

o: Protecting PII in training data, prompts, outputs from unauthorized access
e: GDPR, CCPA. Anonymization, encryption, consent.

o: Maintaining performance with unexpected inputs or adversarial attacks
e: Handles edge cases gracefully. No catastrophic failures.

o: Allocative = unfair resource distribution; representational = stereotypes/erasure
e: Resources (loans, jobs) vs portrayal (stereotypes).

o: Identity/access control specifically for AI agents
e: Agent-specific IAM defining actions and resource access.
Data leakage, output filtering, toxicity, prompt injection defense.

o: RAG grounding + output validation + confidence scoring
e: Layer multiple techniques for best results.
Where from, what happened, where it goes.

q: Match: CloudTrail, Audit Manager, Artifact, Config
e: CloudTrail=API logs. Audit Manager=evidence. Artifact=reports. Config=rules.

o: Framework assessing security requirements by risk level
e: Categorize apps by risk for appropriate controls.
AWS: platform. Customer: IAM, data, PII, regulations.
PrivateLink = private. Traffic stays on AWS network.

o: WHERE data is stored for regulations
e: Geographic requirements: GDPR, sovereignty.

o: Logs all API calls for audit trail
e: Records every Bedrock/SageMaker API call.

o: Protecting stored data/models using encryption when not in use
e: KMS keys protect S3 data, model weights, embeddings.
TLS encrypts client-to-Bedrock and inter-service.

o: Controls who can access AI resources and invoke models
e: Policies control Bedrock access, invocation, KB access.

o: Private connections keeping AI traffic within AWS
e: No internet gateway needed. Compliance requirement.

o: IAM policies determining which users invoke which models
e: Restrict by model ID, region, action.

o: Identifying/redacting personal info in model responses
e: Guardrails/Comprehend find SSN, email, phone, address.
Limits blast radius of compromised credentials.

o: Recording all interactions for compliance and investigation
e: Who, what, when. CloudTrail + CloudWatch.

o: Categorizing by sensitivity for appropriate security controls
e: Public, internal, confidential, restricted.

o: On-demand compliance reports: SOC, PCI, ISO
e: Download compliance docs. Prove AWS compliance to auditors.

o: Automates evidence collection for compliance audits
e: Maps controls to evidence, auto-collects, prepares reports.

o: Discovers PII in S3 buckets used for AI training
e: ML-powered PII discovery. Alerts on exposure risks.
Track settings, alert on non-compliant changes.

o: Tracking deployed versions for rollback and audit
e: Know which version served which predictions.

o: HIPAA agreement for AI processing PHI
e: Legally required for health data. Covers Bedrock, SageMaker.

o: Audit verifying security, availability, privacy controls
e: Auditor verifies controls. Reports via Artifact.

o: Consent, right to explanation, data minimization, deletion
e: Lawful basis, explanation rights, minimization, deletion.

o: Sovereignty = which laws apply; residency = where data must be
e: Laws (sovereignty) vs location (residency). Related but distinct.
